Why Does Half Of Americans Want To Take The Country Back To 
The 1950s 
Inline image I 
Would you rather live in the America of 1950 or the America of 2016? Has the United States changed 
for the better over the last 62 years? Many fondly remember the 1950s and the 1960s as the "golden 
age" of America. We emerged from World War II as the wealthiest and most powerful nation on the 
planet. During that time period, just about anyone that wanted to get a job could find a job and the 
U.S. middle class expanded rapidly. Back in 1950, America was still considered to be a "land of 
opportunity" and the economy was growing like crazy. There was less crime, there was less divorce, 
the American people had much less debt and the world seemed a whole lot less crazy. Most of the rest 
of the world deeply admired us and wanted to be more like us. 
Of course there were a lot of things that were not great about America back in 1950, and there are 
many things that many of us dearly love that we would have to give up in order to go back and live 
during that time. For example, there was no Internet back in 1950. Instead of being able to go online

especially New York, which became a symbol of the new criminality. Though the surge in violence 
affected all the races and both genders, it was most dramatic among black men, whose annual 
homicide rate had shot up by the mid-1980s to 72 per 100,000. 
The flood of violence from the 196os through the 198os reshaped American culture, the political scene, 
and everyday life. Mugger jokes became a staple of comedians, with mentions of Central Park getting 
an instant laugh as a well-known death trap. New Yorkers imprisoned themselves in their apartments 
with batteries of latches and deadbolts, including the popular "police lock," a steel bar with one end 
anchored in the floor and the other propped up against the door. 
The rebounding of violence in the 196os defied every expectation. The decade was a time of 
unprecedented economic growth, nearly full employment, levels of economic equality for which people 
today are nostalgic, historic racial progress, and the blossoming of government social programs, not to 
mention medical advances that made victims more likely to survive being shot or knifed. Social 
theorists in 1962 would have happily bet that these fortunate conditions would lead to a continuing era 
of low crime. And they would have lost their shirts. 
The 196os were supposed to be the era of peace and love, and so they were in some respects. But the 
glorification of dissoluteness shaded into an indulgence of violence and then into violence itself. At the 
end of every concert, The Who famously smashed their instruments to smithereens, which could be 
dismissed as harmless theater were it not for the fact that drummer Keith Moon also destroyed dozens 
of hotel rooms; and accidentally killed his bodyguard by running over him with his car before dying 
himself in 1978 of the customary drug overdose. 
When rock music burst onto the scene in the 195os, politicians and clergymen vilified it for corrupting 
morals and encouraging lawlessness. Do we now have to —

and read the articles that you want to read, your news would have been almost entirely controlled by 
the big media companies of the day. So there are definitely some advantages that we have today that 
they did not have back in 1950. But not all of the changes have been for the better. America is in a 
constant state of change, and many are deeply concerned about where all of these changes are taking 
us. 
There has never been any society in society in the history of the world that has been perfect. America 
was flawed in 1950 just as America is flawed today. And yes in 1950, the United States was #1 in GDP 
per capita while today we are #13 in GDP per capita. Older people may remember 3 cents stamps, or 
when the median age at first marriage was about 22 for men and about 20 for women, Americans 
regularly left their cars and the front doors of their homes unlocked, and when Americans dressed up 
in suits and dresses before getting on an airplane in the 1950s. There was a sense of civility at the time, 
provided that you weren't working in a coal mine in Appalachia, or in the cotton fields in the Deep 
South or were picking grapes in California. And if you were Black, Jewish, Hispanic or gay, more often 
than not, you were treated as a second class citizen in many parts of the country. 
People forget The Cold War, when there were people in the military (on both sides) who believed that 
nuclear wars were winnable. Americans complain about taxes today, but for those of you who 
nostalgic about the 1950s, should remember that in 195o corporate taxes accounted for about 30 
percent of all federal revenue, whereas today corporate taxes will account for less than 7 percent of all 
federal revenue, and the top tax rate for individuals was 91%. I am sure that if the tax rate was the 
same that it was in the 196os the country would have a balanced budget but few people want this. I 
remember the Jim Crowe laws, segregation and the lynching of 14 year-old Emmitt Till, beca

Myths & Facts: Online Exclusive 
Were the Palestinians the Only People Who Became Refugees as a 
Result of the Arab-Israeli Conflict ? 
Although much is heard about the plight of the Palestinian 
refugees, little is said about the Jews who fled from Arab 
states. Their situation had long been precarious. During the 
1947 UN debates, Arab leaders threatened them. For 
example, Egypt's delegate told the General Assembly: "The 
lives of one million Jews in Muslim countries would be 
jeopardized by partition.
-ThjMyths 
& 
Facts: 2012 
The number of Jews fleeing Arab countries for Israel in the 
years following Israel's independence was nearly double the 
number of Arabs leaving Palestine. Many Jews were allowed 
to take little more than the shirts on their backs. These 
refugees had no desire to be repatriated. Little is heard about them because they 
did not remain refugees for long. Of the 820,000 Jewish refugees between 1948 
and 1972, 586,000 were resettled in Israel at great expense, and without any offer 
of compensation from the Arab governments who confiscated their possessions.

Dear Mr. Visoski, 
EFTA00628011

Thank you for your patience. Both Robert Collada, CEO and I have been in Europe with the Boat Shows and have been 
following the progress and conversations with Jerry and Chris. We have brought in our "in-house" Naval Architect, Ms. 
Elisabetta Pianta to finish off the proposal as she is very familiar with this boat. 
The attached drawing we hope reflects all your desires for a replacement boat for Little St. James Island. This is not 
really a fully custom boat but will come from our molded Deep Vee hull 40 foot hull with a modified Cabin 40 deck. 
Point being is that this well proven offshore hull design will be married to our existing molded cabin and flush deck 
design. This will cut down considerably on production time and provide an excellent design. Of course it will be 
finished and detailed to a yacht quality finish. 
We strongly suggest triple YAMAHA F 300 as the weight fully laden with fuel, water, gear and guests require the 
horsepower to punch thru a heavy sea offshore. Less horsepower will place a continuous heavy load on the engines. 
You have the option of trimming up the center engine to use in reserve should you be running very light or at a slow 
cruise. 
We presently have the hull mold inside the factory and can start the build in short order. We are ahead of the rush of 
orders that we usually get during the winter so you are ahead of the que. This is a 6 month build time now but this will 
change very soon as we confirm the other orders which are forthcoming. 
You may choose the color scheme for the boat and how you wish it outfitted. The price quoted is a fully rigged boat as 
shown. Electronics and other special features like underwater lights, bottom paint etc.. are options. Terms are 50% 
deposit with the order, 30% progress payment when the engines are installed and balance upon acceptance at sea-
trial. 
We would like to get your feedback and confirm that we have hit all your targets in this last proposal. Looking forward 
to your reply.

Perry Wong, Belinda Chng, Amos Garcia and Arielle Burstein 
Population aging is a hidden global force with few discernable indicators of its impending social impacts. New

Ed Maguire is Associate Professor and Chair in the Department of Justice, Law & Society at American 
University. He has held previous positions at George Mason University, the University of Nebraska, the 
U.S. Department of Justice, and the United Nations. Professor Maguire's professional interests cover 
a wide range, but most of his work focuses on three topics: police organizations, violent crime, and 
social science measurement. Professor Maguire is invited regularly to speak on topics related to crime 
and justice throughout the United States and around the world. He has written or edited three books 
and more than 40 articles and chapters on various themes related to policing, crime, criminal justice, 
organization theory, and social science methodology. In his spare time, he enjoys carpentry, martial 
arts, and spending time with his family. Degrees -B.S. in Criminal Justice, University of Lowell (1991);
